metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the Remediation to reflect that per-event `process_*` handlers already exist in `_processors.py`; the actual refactoring is replacing the `if/elif` tower (lines 715–756) with a `dict`-keyed dispatch to those existing handlers — no new `_apply_<EVENT>` wrappers are needed.
findings.finding: The plan's central premise — 'move each event-type branch body VERBATIM into its own _apply_<EVENT> handler' — is refuted by reading the file: every branch body in the if/elif tower (lines 715–756) is already a single-line call to an existing named process_* function (e.g., `process_status(state, event, data, filepath)`). There is no inline logic to extract; the per-event handlers already exist. The plan would create a new unnecessary wrapper layer (_apply_X → process_X) rather than a real extract-method refactoring.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'move each event-type branch body VERBATIM into its own _apply_<EVENT> handler'; src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:715–756
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the Remediation paragraph with: 'The per-event handlers (`process_create`, `process_status`, …) already exist. The change is: build a `_DISPATCH` dict mapping each `event_type` string to its handler, then replace the if/elif tower with a single `handler = _DISPATCH.get(event_type)` lookup and call. The CREATE early-return path must be preserved specially (it returns a value; all other handlers return None).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the handler signature contract: specify whether `_apply_<EVENT>` handlers close over outer-loop variables (e.g., `ticket_id`, `cache_path`, `dir_hash`, `tracker_dir`, `event_uuid`, `filepath`) or receive them as additional parameters — and update the AC to reflect whichever approach is chosen.
findings.finding: The plan specifies handler signature `_apply_<EVENT>(state, event)`, but several branches in the existing dispatch tower call `process_*` functions that require additional positional arguments beyond `(state, event, data)`: `process_create` needs `ticket_id, cache_path, dir_hash`; `process_link` needs `tracker_dir`; `process_bridge_alert`/`process_revert` need `event_uuid`; `process_status` needs `filepath`. The plan does not specify how these outer-loop variables will be threaded into the extracted handlers, leaving the handler contract underspecified.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'move each event-type branch body VERBATIM into its own `_apply_<EVENT>(state, event)` handler'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) extend the handler signature to `_apply_<EVENT>(state, event, data, **ctx)` where `ctx` carries per-handler extra args, or (b) explicitly note that handlers are closures over the `replay_events` frame. Both are valid; the plan should state the choice.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the dispatch table points directly to existing `process_*` functions rather than introducing a new `_apply_<EVENT>` wrapper layer (the branch bodies are already extracted; only a dict mapping `event_type -> process_fn` is needed, with a thin adapter only for CREATE's non-None return path).
findings.finding: The plan calls for introducing new `_apply_<EVENT>` wrapper functions, but the event-branch bodies in replay_events are ALREADY one-to-two-line delegations to existing `process_*` functions (process_create, process_status, etc.) that are extracted, documented, and publicly re-exported. Adding a `_apply_*` naming layer on top would be a premature abstraction with exactly zero call-sites beyond the single dispatch table — a Rule-of-Three violation (1 call-site vs. required >=3).
findings.location: Remediation section: 'move each event-type branch body VERBATIM into its own `_apply_<EVENT>(state, event)` handler'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the if/elif tower with a dispatch dict: `_HANDLERS = {'STATUS': process_status, 'COMMENT': process_comment, ...}` and a loop body like `handler = _HANDLERS.get(event_type); if handler: handler(state, event, data)` (with CREATE's early-return handled separately as the single special case). No new `_apply_*` functions needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the mechanism: since branch bodies are already one-line `process_*` callouts, use a direct dict-dispatch table (`DISPATCH = {'CREATE': process_create, ...}`) rather than introducing a new `_apply_*` wrapper layer — this achieves the same depth flattening with no added indirection.
findings.finding: The plan instructs 'move each event-type branch body VERBATIM into its own _apply_<EVENT> handler', but the branch bodies in the if/elif tower are already one-line callouts to existing process_* functions — there is no substantial body to extract. Introducing a new _apply_* wrapper layer around the already-existing process_* layer adds pointless indirection. A direct dict-dispatch to the existing process_* functions achieves the same depth reduction more cleanly.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'move each event-type branch body VERBATIM into its own _apply_<EVENT>(state, event) handler'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the extract-method step with: build a module-level dict `_DISPATCH: dict[str, Callable] = {'STATUS': process_status, 'COMMENT': process_comment, ...}` mapping event_type to the existing process_* function. The loop body becomes `handler = _DISPATCH.get(event_type); if handler: handler(state, event, data, ...)`. The CREATE early-return and forward-compat pass cases are handled explicitly before/after the dict lookup.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all outer-scope locals referenced by each branch body in `replay_events` and specify whether they are added to the handler signature, converted to a context object, or confirmed absent — before declaring the extraction 'VERBATIM' and 'behavior-identical'.
findings.finding: The plan declares handler bodies will be extracted 'VERBATIM' and the refactoring will be 'behavior-identical', but method extraction is not truly verbatim if the branch bodies in `replay_events` reference local variables from the outer scope beyond just `state` and `event`. The plan specifies only `_apply_<EVENT>(state, event)` as the handler signature, leaving unaddressed how any additional outer-scope locals (e.g., loop variables, intermediate accumulators, config lookups computed before the dispatch) are threaded into handlers. A missed outer-local becomes a NameError or a subtle behavioral drift.
findings.location: Remediation section / Acceptance Criteria: 'Each handler holds the same logic as its former branch'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step: 'Audit each branch body for references to outer-scope locals in `replay_events`; either (a) pass them as explicit additional parameters to the handler, (b) bundle them into a `ReplayContext` dataclass passed alongside `state` and `event`, or (c) confirm no such references exist. Document the chosen signature in the plan.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit note in the plan that the CREATE handler's early-return semantics must be preserved in the new dispatch loop (e.g., check the return value of the CREATE handler specifically, or keep CREATE as a pre-dispatch special case before the uniform dispatch table).
findings.finding: The CREATE handler is the only branch that returns a non-None value (early-exit on corrupt CREATE), making its call-site asymmetric with every other handler. The plan does not address how the dispatch loop handles this special case when the tower is replaced by a uniform dispatch mechanism.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'replace the nested if/elif tower with a dispatch that calls the handler by event type' / AC line 1 'replay_events is a shallow dispatch loop'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a golden-state snapshot test that serializes `compiled_state` before and after the refactor for a representative multi-event fixture, or replace the 'byte-identical' AC language with 'all existing reducer replay tests pass' to match what the test suite actually verifies.
findings.finding: The plan's AC states 'Reducer replay tests pass unchanged (byte-identical final state before/after)' but no test in the test suite was found that asserts byte-identical compiled state as a regression guard. The existing tests verify behavioral correctness field-by-field; none perform a serialized-output snapshot comparison that would catch dict key ordering or other subtle byte-level differences introduced by a dispatch refactor.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Reducer replay tests pass unchanged (byte-identical final state before/after)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the Remediation and AC2 language: either change Remediation to 'byte-identical' or weaken AC2 to 'behaviorally equivalent output' and specify how equivalence is measured.
findings.finding: Terminology drift between 'Behavior-identical' in Remediation and 'byte-identical final state' in AC2 — these are different standards: behavior-identical allows observationally equivalent but non-identical output, while byte-identical is a stricter guarantee. An implementer must know which bar to meet.
findings.location: Remediation section ('Behavior-identical') vs Acceptance Criteria AC2 ('byte-identical final state')
findings.suggested_fix: For a deterministic reducer over discrete events with no floating-point or ordering nondeterminism, 'byte-identical' is the right bar. Change Remediation's 'Behavior-identical' to 'byte-identical output' for consistency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC1's depth bound and remove the tilde, e.g. `python -c "import ast, sys; ..." src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` or `radon cc -s src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py | grep replay_events` with an explicit max depth (e.g. <=4).
findings.finding: AC1 ('depth <= ~4') has no concrete proving command for nesting depth, and the tilde makes the bound fuzzy; an agent cannot objectively verify the criterion is met without a specified measurement tool.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(depth <= ~4)' with '(depth <= 4)' and add: `radon cc -n B src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` must produce no entry for `replay_events` (complexity A/B means nesting <=4), or equivalently `python -m mccabe --min 5 src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` must emit no output for `replay_events`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for byte identity, e.g. capture `json.dumps(final_state, sort_keys=True)` before and after and assert hash equality, OR downgrade the claim to 'all replay tests pass' if byte identity is not actually required.
findings.finding: AC2 claims 'byte-identical final state before/after' but provides no proving command that verifies byte identity; the referenced 'reducer replay tests' likely assert logical correctness, not serialized byte equality.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add: `pytest tests/reducer/ -q && python scripts/replay_hash_check.py` (a script that replays a canonical fixture and compares SHA256 of sorted JSON output), or change AC2 to 'Reducer replay tests pass unchanged' without the byte-identical qualifier.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or supplement AC3 with an observable, testable criterion, e.g. 'git diff of each handler body shows only whitespace/indentation changes relative to its original branch' or 'a per-event fixture test (one per event type) passes for both old and new code paths'.
findings.finding: AC3 ('Each handler holds the same logic as its former branch') is effort-framed and subjective — it describes what the developer should do, not a verifiable observable end-state, and has no proving command.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'For each event type, a targeted replay fixture test exists and passes (or the existing suite covers all event types); `git diff HEAD~1 -- src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` shows no net logic change in handler bodies (only extraction and dispatch scaffolding added).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and cite the tool/metric that produces the depth-19 figure for `replay_events` (e.g., `radon cc`, `flake8-bugbear` complexity score, or a specific nesting-depth linter); if the actual nesting depth is ~6, revise the premise or clarify that the metric is cyclomatic complexity / McCabe score, not raw nesting depth.
findings.finding: The plan asserts a nesting depth of 19 for `replay_events`, but reading the function (lines 648–761) the maximum control-flow nesting is approximately 6–7 levels (for → try → post-try conditionals → if/elif → inner if-result). The depth-19 figure is unverified by the code and appears incorrect.
findings.location: Finding section — 'nesting depth 19 (deepest in repo)'; src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:648

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale for the dict-dispatch approach (e.g., 'a module-level dispatch dict maps directly onto the already-extracted process_* functions, requires zero new abstractions, and is the idiomatic Python pattern for event-type routing — keeping blast radius to a single function and one new constant').
findings.finding: The plan gives 'Behavior-identical (handler bodies are the same code; dispatch selects the same branch)' as its sole rationale, which is a safety argument, not a positive rationale for WHY dispatch-table is the right structural choice for this codebase. A positive rationale is required by the criterion.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'Behavior-identical (handler bodies are the same code; dispatch selects the same branch). Guarded by the reducer's replay tests'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
